summary: Charity Majors recounts how Linden Lab's tradition of wearing Shrek ears after breaking production created a psychologically safe culture around failure long before 'blameless retrospectives' existed as a concept. The opt-in ritual transformed what could be shame into celebration, teaching engineers that failure is inevitable, constant, and even funny. While acknowledging the practice might not translate to modern workplaces, she argues it was a brilliant piece of social engineering that made transparency about mistakes infectious across the entire company.
key_points: Linden Lab's Shrek ears tradition predated and functionally served as 'blameless retrospectives' before the industry developed that language
The ritual was opt-in and spread organically beyond engineering to sales, marketing, and accounting through voluntary adoption
Failure was reframed as inevitable and even celebrated, especially in a pre-DevOps era running massively distributed systems without modern tooling
The practice taught three core lessons: it is safe to fail, failure is constant, and failure is hilarious
Modern sensibilities rightly make such practices harder to implement today, which Majors sees as a sign of progress in workplace culture
money_quotes: The Shrek ears were a brilliant piece of social jujitsu.
Failure is both inevitable and ridiculous, and no one is going to get mad at you or fire you when it happens.
If you never break production, you probably aren't doing anything that really matters or taking enough risks.
The Shrek ears taught us that you fail, you laugh, you drink whiskey, you move on.
Making something goofy is the best way to make it stick.
key_insight: Workplace rituals that transform failure from shameful to celebratory are powerful tools for building psychologically safe engineering cultures, but they must evolve as our understanding of workplace dynamics matures.
